---
title: "Quantile & Distributional Methods"
subtitle: "Beyond the conditional mean"
---

## Overview

Almost everything so far has been about averages --- the mean effect, the average treatment effect, the conditional expectation. But averages hide as much as they reveal. A reform might leave the *mean* HbA1c unchanged while sharply helping the worst-controlled patients and slightly harming the best-controlled. A wage policy can lift the bottom of the distribution and compress the top. To see these patterns you must model the whole distribution, not just its centre.

This module develops the distributional toolkit. **Quantile regression** lets a covariate have different effects at different points of the outcome distribution. **Distributional treatment effects** compare entire treated and control distributions, revealing winners and losers that a mean effect of zero would conceal. And **decomposition methods** (Oaxaca-Blinder and beyond) split a gap between groups into a part explained by their characteristics and a part attributable to different returns --- the workhorse of the inequality and discrimination literatures.

## Pages in This Module

- [Quantile Regression](quantile-regression.qmd) --- Modeling the whole conditional distribution, not just the mean.
- [Distributional Treatment Effects](distributional-effects.qmd) --- Effects beyond the average.
- [Decompositions: Oaxaca-Blinder and Beyond](decompositions.qmd) --- Explained versus unexplained gaps.
